retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Technical Elements of Images Acquisition - Technical acquisition of radiographs depends on the anatomic body part being imaged. Radiology studies are generally comprised of 3 or more views; however, a detailed description of patient positioning and required views for specific radiographic studies is beyond the scope of this topic. In general, musculoskeletal examinations comprise anteroposterior, lateral, and oblique views with additional unique views for various body parts such as the Grashey and axillary views of the shoulder or the Judet view of the pelvis. The following represent basic instructions for classic anteroposterior (AP) views of common anatomic studies obtained in radiology with collimation values adapted from the 14th edition of Merril’s atlas of radiographic positions and radiologic procedures:

retrieved_docs.title: Acetabular Fractures -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of acetabular fracture is possible just with a plain X-ray, but because many patients have multiple organ injuries, a CT scan is often necessary, which is more precise than a conventional X-ray.  Plain films, with an AP pelvis and Judet views, are often obtained first.  The Judet views include an obturator oblique and an iliac oblique view.  There are six radiographic landmarks identifiable on an AP view of the pelvis and help classify the fracture pattern. [6] The landmarks are: Iliopectineal line Ilioischial line Teardrop Roof of acetabulum Anterior wall Posterior wall

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Foot Bones -- Clinical Significance -- Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: The talar neck has less trabecular bone than the head and body and is relatively weak. The most common mechanism of fracture is via forced dorsiflexion, and the first case series was described in WWI fighter pilots, termed aviator astragalus; 100 years later, they occur mostly from motor vehicle collisions or falls. Talar neck fractures classify according to the Hawkins-Canale system, and there is a strong correlation with fracture classification and avascular necrosis. [6] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Acetabular Fractures -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Acetabular fractures primarily occur in young people who are involved in high-velocity trauma. Since the advent of mandatory seatbelt use, there has been a significant reduction of acetabular fractures to approximately an incidence of 3 per 100000. There has been an increase in the number of acetabulum fractures resulting from a fall of fewer than 10 feet, likely due to the increase in osteopenia/osteoporosis. Little has changed since Letournel and Judet’s landmark paper in 1993, and many of their findings remain the “gold standard” for treatment today. Among the most significant advancements has been the advent of percutaneous fixation of certain fracture types. This activity outlines the evaluation, diagnosis, and treatment of patients with acetabulum fractures. It also reviews the role of pre, peri, and post-operative management of acetabulum fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: PENG Regional Block(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: With the patient in the supine position, the ultrasound probe is placed on a transverse plane over the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS). Once the ASIS is identified, the transducer is aligned with the pubic ramus and rotated at approximately 45 degrees, parallel to the inguinal crease. The transducer is then slid medially along this axis until the anterior inferior iliac spine (AIIS), iliopubic eminence (IPE), and the psoas tendon is clearly identified, serving as anatomic landmarks.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Hip Dislocation -- Evaluation -- X-rays
retrieved_docs.content: On a normal AP pelvis, the femoral heads should appear similar in size with symmetric joint spaces. The joint with an anterior dislocation will project a larger-appearing femoral head. A femoral neck fracture should be ruled out by this image prior to attempting reduction. Judet views (45 degree internal and external oblique views) may be of some help in evaluating for bone fragments and occult acetabular and femoral head and neck fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: CT pelvimetry is a primary imaging modality that is mapped to pelvimetrie, serving as the main method for measuring pelvic dimensions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 5 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis is classified as a CT scan abdomen, representing a focused imaging study of the abdominal region. CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis is also derived from the CT scan abdomen, indicating that it includes abdominal imaging as part of a broader examination that also covers the chest and pelvis. Both procedures share the core component of abdominal CT imaging, with the former being a dedicated abdominal and pelvic scan and the latter extending the coverage to include the thoracic cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2624
retrieved_docs.content: Open repair of kidney or renal pelvis is a surgical repair method used to address injuries or defects of the kidney or renal pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2155
retrieved_docs.content: CT of regions is classified as a subcategory of فحص الحوض بالتصوير المقطعي المحوسب, indicating that it represents a specific type or component within a pelvic computed tomography examination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 111
retrieved_docs.content: and all imaging procedures are linked to a standardized diagnostic/staging answer set (PhenX), supporting their use in patient assessment and research phenotyping. This integrated hierarchy enables clinicians to select appropriate modalities, understand methodological bases, and navigate relationships among bone density testing, plain radiography, and CT across chest, abdomen, pelvis, retroperitoneum, genitourinary, and central nervous system for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis and Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are specific imaging procedures that are categorized as “Other CT scan.” These “Other CT scan” examinations fall under the broader imaging modality referred to as tomografie, which encompasses various computed tomography techniques used to visualize abdominal and pelvic structures, with contrast enhancement providing additional diagnostic detail.

Finally, procedures involving the retroperitoneal space demonstrate site‑specific relationships: removal of a tube from the retroperitoneum is an indirect therapeutic procedure, while diagnostic ultrasound of the abdomen or retroperitoneum is a direct diagnostic procedure targeting the same compartment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: the kidneys, retroperitoneum, pelvis, chest and abdomen to broader genitourinary and retrograde urinary imaging categories, while also defining the methodological role of computed tomography across listed and unlisted procedures and its integration with MRI‑based and CT‑based computer‑assisted surgical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2150
retrieved_docs.content: Radiologic procedures on the chest and abdomen encompass a range of imaging techniques, and within this broader category the specific modality of radiography of the retroperitoneum is classified as a child procedure, representing a focused imaging approach to evaluate structures located behind the peritoneal cavity as part of comprehensive thoraco‑abdominal radiologic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 991
retrieved_docs.content: radiology to retrograde urinary studies. CT pelvimetry is directly mapped to “pelvimetrie,” and “Other chest X‑ray” is a child of the Arabic‑language modality “التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر” (computed tomography of the chest). Together, these relationships illustrate how general diagnostic radiology categories contain specific imaging modalities (CT, X‑ray, soft‑tissue X‑ray) and procedural families (genitourinary, retroperitoneal, pelvic), with computed tomography imaging‑action serving as the methodological foundation for both listed and unlisted CT procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis and Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are both classified as “Other CT scan,” indicating that each of these imaging procedures is considered an alternative or supplementary CT examination derived from a broader CT scan category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2679
retrieved_docs.content: The retroperitoneální prostor is a key anatomical region involved in both imaging and interventional procedures. A diagnostic ultrasound of the abdomen or retroperitoneum directly targets the retroperitoneální prostor, providing real‑time visualization of structures within this space. Conversely, the removal of a tube from the retroperitoneum is performed as an indirect procedure related to the retroperitoneální prostor, reflecting its role in therapeutic interventions that access or modify contents of this deep abdominal compartment. Together, these relationships underscore the retroperitoneální prostor’s importance as both a direct site for diagnostic imaging and an indirect site for procedural management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1951
retrieved_docs.content: The kidney and pelvis are closely linked to other non‑operative therapeutic procedures of the urinary tract, indicating that interventions involving these renal structures are categorized within the broader group of non‑surgical urinary tract procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging study that can be directly linked to several more advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is primarily mapped to both “CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis” and “Computerized tomography of chest,” indicating that these comprehensive and focused chest CT scans are the main follow‑up modalities when further anatomic detail is required beyond the plain radiograph. Additionally, routine chest x-ray is associated with other, less primary CT options, including “Computerized tomography of brain and chest” and “Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium.” These alternative mappings reflect situations where clinicians may extend evaluation to include intracranial structures or require contrast enhancement for enhanced tissue characterization. Together, these relationships illustrate how a routine chest x-ray can guide the selection of targeted or extensive CT imaging, supporting diagnostic decision‑making and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: The CT scan of the abdomen (ct scan abdomen) is a specific type of combined CT of the chest and abdomen (CT of chest and abdomen), indicating that it falls under the broader category of thoraco‑abdominal computed tomography. Clinically, the ct scan abdomen is associated with abdominal ultrasound imaging (تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية), reflecting that both modalities are commonly employed together to evaluate abdominal pathology and complement each other in patient assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 480
retrieved_docs.content: tomography imaging, and shared anatomical targets. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding the placement of each imaging modality within the larger diagnostic radiology framework, facilitating appropriate selection and interpretation of studies for patient care.
